1:
"And Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old: these were the years of the life of Sarah." The witnesses note the age and life of Sarah. On "Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old," the grammatical voices note the years. Ellicott and Gill note that "Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old: these were the years of the life of Sarah" — Sarah's full age was a hundred and twenty-seven years; she is the only woman in Scripture whose age at death is recorded; the honor given to the matriarch of the covenant; the years of her life summed up.

On "these were the years of the life of Sarah," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the honor. Henry and Calvin note that the special recording of Sarah's age and life ("these were the years of the life of Sarah") marks the honor due to her — the wife of Abraham, the mother of the promised seed, the matriarch of Israel (Isaiah 51:2, "look unto Abraham your father, and unto Sarah that bare you"); the dignity of Sarah, commemorated; the mother of the faithful (1 Peter 3:6).

On Sarah's life, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the matriarch: Sarah's long life (127 years), thirty-seven years after the birth of Isaac (he being now thirty-seven); the honored matriarch; the recording of her years a token of her place in the covenant history; the close of Sarah's life, the first death in the patriarchal family recorded. The verse displays the age and life of Sarah.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the years — "Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old," the only woman's age recorded, the honor to the matriarch (Ellicott, Gill); "these were the years of the life of Sarah," the dignity of Sarah commemorated (Isaiah 51:2; 1 Peter 3:6, Henry, Calvin); the honored matriarch (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the aged Sarah. The verse marks the age: 127. The verse displays the life: summed up. The verse displays the honor: to Sarah. The verse displays the matriarch: of the covenant. The recording displays the honor due to Sarah. The verse displays the Sarah: 127. The verse displays the years: recorded. The verse displays the dignity: marked. The age and life of Sarah display the honor to the matriarch: Sarah was a hundred and twenty-seven years old, the years of her life — the only woman whose age is recorded, the honor due to the mother of the promised seed (Isaiah 51:2; 1 Peter 3:6). The verse displays the aged Sarah, who dies at Hebron (v.2).
2:
"And Sarah died in Kirjatharba; the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan: and Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her." The witnesses note Sarah's death and Abraham's mourning. On "Sarah died in Kirjatharba; the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan," the grammatical voices note the death. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Sarah died in Kirjatharba (the city of Arba); the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan" — Sarah died at Hebron (Kirjath-arba, "the city of Arba," a notable Hittite/Anakite town); the death of the matriarch in the land of promise (where Abraham sojourned); the first death recorded in the patriarchal family.

On "Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the mourning. Henry and Calvin note that "Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her" — the patriarch mourned and wept for his wife; the genuine grief of Abraham for his beloved companion of so many years; the lawful, natural sorrow of the godly at the death of loved ones (not the hopeless grief of the world, 1 Thessalonians 4:13, but real grief); the affection of Abraham for Sarah.

On the mourning, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the example: Abraham's mourning and weeping show that the godly may and do grieve at the death of the dear; the natural affection sanctified by faith; the patriarch's sorrow, yet (as the chapter shows) joined with the hope of the resurrection (burying his dead in the land of promise); the example of godly grief, tempered by hope. The verse displays Sarah's death and Abraham's mourning.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the death — "Sarah died in Kirjatharba; the same is Hebron," Sarah's death at Hebron, in the land of promise (Keil, Gill); "Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her," the patriarch's genuine grief (Henry, Calvin); the godly grief tempered by hope (1 Thessalonians 4:13, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dead Sarah. The verse marks the death: at Hebron. The verse displays the place: Kirjath-arba. The verse displays the mourning: of Abraham. The verse displays the grief: genuine. The death displays Sarah's passing and Abraham's sorrow. The verse displays the Sarah: died. The verse displays the Abraham: mourning. The verse displays the grief: real. Sarah's death and Abraham's mourning display the godly grief tempered by hope: Sarah died at Hebron, and Abraham came to mourn and weep for her — the death of the matriarch in the land of promise, and the patriarch's genuine, affectionate grief, the lawful sorrow of the godly (1 Thessalonians 4:13). The verse displays the mourning Abraham, who seeks a burying-place (vv.3-4).
3:
"And Abraham stood up from before his dead, and spake unto the sons of Heth, saying," The brief verse notes Abraham rising to seek a burying-place from the sons of Heth. The grammatical voices note the approach. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham stood up from before his dead, and spake unto the sons of Heth" — Abraham rose from beside the body of Sarah (his "dead") and went to speak to the sons of Heth (the Hittites, the inhabitants of Hebron) about a burying-place; the patriarch, in his grief, attending to the burial; the dealing with the people of the land for a tomb.

On "stood up from before his dead," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the duty. Henry and Calvin note that Abraham "stood up from before his dead" — turning from his mourning to the duty of burial; the care of the godly for the decent burial of the dead (a token of respect for the body and of the hope of the resurrection); the patriarch rising from grief to action.

On "spake unto the sons of Heth," the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the dealing: "spake unto the sons of Heth" — Abraham addressed the Hittites (the people of the land, the children of Heth, 10:15) to obtain a burying-place; the courteous, careful dealing with the inhabitants; the seeking of a possession (a tomb) in the land of promise; the negotiation for the cave of Machpelah (vv.4-16). The verse displays Abraham rising to seek a burying-place.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the approach — "Abraham stood up from before his dead, and spake unto the sons of Heth," Abraham rising to seek a tomb from the Hittites (Ellicott, Gill); "stood up from before his dead," turning from grief to the duty of burial (Henry, Calvin); "spake unto the sons of Heth," the dealing with the people of the land (10:15, JFB, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rising Abraham. The verse marks the rising: from his dead. The verse displays the sons of Heth: addressed. The verse displays the burial: sought. The verse displays the duty: attended. The approach displays Abraham seeking a tomb in the land. The verse displays the Abraham: rising. The verse displays the Hittites: addressed. The verse displays the burying-place: sought. Abraham rising to seek a burying-place from the sons of Heth displays the care of the godly for burial: Abraham rose from before his dead and spoke to the sons of Heth — the patriarch turning from grief to the duty of burial, dealing with the people of the land for a tomb. The verse displays the dealing Abraham, calling himself a stranger (v.4).
4:
"I am a stranger and a sojourner with you: give me a possession of a buryingplace with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight." The witnesses note Abraham's confession as a stranger and pilgrim, seeking a burying-place — the crux of the pilgrim and the possession. On "I am a stranger and a sojourner with you," interpreters note the confession. The grammatical-historical Keil and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke note that Abraham said, "I am a stranger and a sojourner with you" — the patriarch confessed himself a stranger and sojourner (a resident alien) in the land; though it was the land of promise (to be his seed's inheritance), he possessed none of it (Acts 7:5) and dwelt as a pilgrim; the confession of faith — Abraham and the patriarchs "confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth," seeking "a better country, that is, an heavenly" (Hebrews 11:13-16); the pilgrim character of the man of faith.

On "give me a possession of a buryingplace with you," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the possession. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham sought "a possession of a buryingplace" — to buy a tomb, his own possession, in the land; notably, the only piece of the promised land Abraham would possess was a grave (the cave of Machpelah, vv.9, 17-20); yet the buying of a burying-place in the land was an act of faith — a pledge and earnest of the future inheritance, and a confession of the hope of the resurrection (laying his dead in the land God had promised to his seed, in expectation of rising in it); the stranger seeking a possession in the land of promise.

On "that I may bury my dead out of my sight," the grammatical-historical note the burial: "that I may bury my dead out of my sight" — Abraham sought to bury Sarah decently; the care for the body; the seeking of a tomb in the land; the burial in faith and hope. The verse displays Abraham's confession as a stranger and his seeking a possession.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abraham, confessing himself a stranger and sojourner, sought to buy a burying-place in the land (Keil, Clarke, Calvin, Gill). The confession bears its weight as a crux of the pilgrim and the possession: Abraham a stranger and pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13), possessing none of the land (Acts 7:5), yet buying a burying-place — his only possession in the land of promise a grave, the purchase an act of faith, a pledge of the future inheritance and a confession of the hope of the resurrection. The verse displays the pilgrim-and-possession crux. The verse marks the confession: a stranger and sojourner. The verse displays the possession: a burying-place. The verse displays the pilgrim: Abraham. The verse displays the hope: of the inheritance. The confession displays Abraham the pilgrim seeking a possession in faith. The verse displays the Abraham: a stranger. The verse displays the possession: sought. The verse displays the hope: of resurrection. Abraham's confession as a stranger and pilgrim, seeking a burying-place, displays his faith and a crux: I am a stranger and a sojourner; give me a possession of a burying-place — Abraham confessing himself a pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13), possessing none of the land (Acts 7:5), yet buying a grave, his only possession in the land of promise, an act of faith, a pledge of the inheritance and the hope of the resurrection. The verse displays the pilgrim Abraham, honored by the Hittites (vv.5-6).
5:
"And the children of Heth answered Abraham, saying unto him," The very brief verse introduces the Hittites' courteous answer to Abraham. The grammatical voices note the response. Gill notes that "the children of Heth answered Abraham, saying unto him" — the Hittites (the people of the land, v.3) responded to Abraham's request (v.4) with respect and courtesy; the introduction to their honorable answer (v.6); the people of the land dealing kindly with the patriarch.

On "the children of Heth answered Abraham," the witnesses note the courtesy. Gill notes that the Hittites answered Abraham courteously and respectfully; the favor and esteem in which the patriarch was held among them (a mighty prince, v.6); the honorable dealing of the people of the land with the stranger.

On the answer, the grammatical-historical note the respect: the children of Heth's answer (vv.5-6) shows the respect and goodwill the Hittites bore Abraham; the providence giving the patriarch favor among the people of the land (as with Abimelech, 21:22); the courteous negotiation for the burying-place. The verse introduces the Hittites' answer to Abraham.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the response — "the children of Heth answered Abraham, saying unto him," the Hittites' courteous response (Gill); the favor and esteem for the patriarch (Gill); the respect of the people of the land (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the answering Hittites. The verse marks the answer: of the Hittites. The verse displays the courtesy: shown. The verse displays the Abraham: esteemed. The verse displays the response: honorable. The answer displays the Hittites dealing courteously with Abraham. Solomon notes that when a man's ways please the LORD, he maketh even his enemies to be at peace with him (Proverbs 16:7), and the favour shown Abraham by the people of the land was of God's overruling providence, as Abimelech too had owned (Genesis 21:22). The verse displays the children of Heth: answering. The verse displays the respect: shown. The verse displays the favor: given. The Hittites' courteous answer to Abraham displays the favor he had among the people of the land: the children of Heth answered Abraham — the people of the land responding to the patriarch's request with respect and goodwill (v.6). The verse displays the answering Hittites, honoring Abraham (v.6).
6:
"Hear us, my lord: thou art a mighty prince among us: in the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead; none of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre, but that thou mayest bury thy dead." The witnesses note the Hittites honoring Abraham as a mighty prince. On "thou art a mighty prince among us," the grammatical voices note the honor. Ellicott and Gill note that "thou art a mighty prince among us (a prince of God)" — the Hittites honored Abraham as "a mighty prince" (literally, "a prince of God," a great and honored man, blessed of God); the esteem in which the patriarch was held among the people of the land; the providence giving Abraham favor and dignity (as Abimelech had owned, 21:22); the recognition of God's blessing on him.

On "in the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the offer. Calvin and Gill note that the Hittites offered Abraham the choice of their sepulchres — "in the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead"; the generous offer of any tomb he wished; the honor and goodwill of the people; the free offer of a burying-place.

On "none of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the goodwill: "none of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre" — none would refuse Abraham a tomb; the unanimous goodwill of the Hittites; the honor done to the patriarch; the favor that opened the way for the purchase of Machpelah (vv.7-16). The verse displays the Hittites honoring Abraham as a mighty prince.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the honor — "thou art a mighty prince among us," the Hittites honoring Abraham as a prince of God (21:22, Ellicott, Gill); "in the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead," the generous offer (Calvin, Gill); "none of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre," the unanimous goodwill (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the honored Abraham. The verse marks the prince: of God. The verse displays the offer: any sepulchre. The verse displays the goodwill: unanimous. The verse displays the honor: shown. The answer displays the Hittites honoring and offering. The verse displays the Abraham: honored. The verse displays the sepulchre: offered. The verse displays the favor: given. The Hittites honoring Abraham as a mighty prince display the favor of God on the patriarch: thou art a mighty prince among us; bury thy dead in the choice of our sepulchres — the Hittites honoring Abraham (a prince of God) and freely offering a tomb, the favor that opened the way for the purchase. The verse displays the honored Abraham, who bows and asks for Machpelah (vv.7-9).
7:
"And Abraham stood up, and bowed himself to the people of the land, even to the children of Heth." The very brief verse notes Abraham's courteous bowing to the people of the land. The grammatical voices note the courtesy. Gill and Calvin note that "Abraham stood up, and bowed himself to the people of the land, even to the children of Heth" — Abraham, in response to the Hittites' honorable offer (v.6), rose and bowed to them in courteous acknowledgment; the patriarch's politeness and humility; the gracious dealing of Abraham with the people of the land; the courtesy of the godly man.

On "bowed himself to the people of the land," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the humility. Wesley and Gill note that Abraham "bowed himself to the people of the land" — a token of respect and gratitude for their goodwill; the humility and courtesy of the great patriarch toward the Hittites; the godly man honoring others (Romans 12:10); the gracious manners of Abraham.

On the bowing, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the dealing: Abraham's bowing prepared for his renewed request (to buy the cave of Machpelah from Ephron, vv.8-9); the courteous negotiation; the patriarch's humble, gracious dealing in obtaining the burying-place; the example of courtesy and humility. The verse displays Abraham's courteous bowing to the people of the land.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the courtesy — "Abraham stood up, and bowed himself to the people of the land," Abraham's courteous bowing in acknowledgment (Gill, Calvin); "bowed himself to the people of the land," the humility and respect of the patriarch (Romans 12:10, Wesley, Gill); the courteous dealing preparing for the request (vv.8-9, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bowing Abraham. The verse marks the bowing: to the Hittites. The verse displays the courtesy: shown. The verse displays the humility: of Abraham. The verse displays the dealing: gracious. The bowing displays Abraham's courteous acknowledgment. The verse displays the Abraham: bowing. The verse displays the people: honored. The verse displays the courtesy: shown. Abraham's courteous bowing to the people of the land displays his humility and grace: Abraham rose and bowed to the children of Heth — the patriarch acknowledging their goodwill with courteous respect, the humility of the godly man (Romans 12:10). The verse displays the bowing Abraham, who asks for Ephron's cave (vv.8-9).
8:
"And he communed with them, saying, If it be your mind that I should bury my dead out of my sight; hear me, and intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar," The brief verse notes Abraham asking the Hittites to intercede with Ephron. The grammatical voices note the request. Gill and Clarke note that "he communed with them, saying, If it be your mind that I should bury my dead out of my sight; hear me, and intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar" — Abraham asked the Hittites, if they were willing he should bury his dead, to intercede for him with Ephron (the owner of the cave he desired); the courteous request through the people of the land; the patriarch's careful, honorable dealing.

On "If it be your mind," the witnesses note the courtesy. Gill notes that Abraham asked their goodwill ("if it be your mind") — the courteous seeking of their consent and help; the gracious dealing of the patriarch; the asking of the people's favor.

On "intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the request: "intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar" — Abraham asked them to entreat (intercede with) Ephron, the owner of the cave of Machpelah (v.9), to sell it to him; the seeking of the specific tomb through the mediation of the people; the courteous, careful negotiation for the burying-place; the patriarch's particular desire for Machpelah. The verse displays Abraham asking the Hittites to intercede with Ephron.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the request — "intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar," Abraham asking the Hittites to intercede with Ephron (Gill, Clarke); "if it be your mind," the courteous seeking of their consent (Gill); the negotiation for the cave of Machpelah (v.9, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the requesting Abraham. The verse marks the request: intreat for me. The verse displays the Ephron: the owner. The verse displays the courtesy: shown. The verse displays the cave: desired. The request displays Abraham seeking Machpelah through the people. The verse displays the Abraham: requesting. The verse displays the Ephron: to be entreated. The verse displays the cave: sought. Abraham asking the Hittites to intercede with Ephron displays his courteous dealing: if it be your mind, entreat for me to Ephron — the patriarch seeking, through the goodwill of the people, to buy the cave of Machpelah from its owner (v.9). The verse displays the requesting Abraham, naming the cave (v.9).
9:
"That he may give me the cave of Machpelah, which he hath, which is in the end of his field; for as much money as it is worth he shall give it me for a possession of a buryingplace amongst you." The witnesses note Abraham seeking to buy the cave of Machpelah at full price. On "the cave of Machpelah, which he hath, which is in the end of his field," the grammatical voices note the cave. Ellicott and Gill note that "the cave of Machpelah, which he hath, which is in the end of his field" — Abraham named the specific tomb he desired: the cave of Machpelah (the "double" cave), belonging to Ephron, at the end of his field; the patriarch's particular request; the cave that would become the burying-place of the patriarchs (the tomb of Sarah, Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, Leah, 49:31; 50:13).

On "for as much money as it is worth," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the full price. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham would buy the cave "for as much money as it is worth" — at the full, fair price; he would not take it as a gift (v.11) but pay its value; the honest, scrupulous dealing of the patriarch, that the possession might be securely and rightfully his (against future dispute); the integrity of Abraham in the purchase.

On "for a possession of a buryingplace amongst you," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the possession: "for a possession of a buryingplace amongst you" — Abraham sought the cave as a secure possession (a permanent burying-place, his own); the first possession in the land of promise (a grave, vv.17-20); the pledge of the future inheritance; the burying-place of the patriarchs in the land. The verse displays Abraham seeking to buy the cave of Machpelah at full price.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cave — "the cave of Machpelah, which he hath, which is in the end of his field," the specific tomb desired, the burying-place of the patriarchs (49:31, Ellicott, Gill); "for as much money as it is worth," the full price, the honest dealing (Calvin, Gill); "for a possession of a buryingplace," the secure possession, the first in the land (vv.17-20, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the cave sought. The verse marks the cave: Machpelah. The verse displays the price: full. The verse displays the possession: secure. The verse displays the burying-place: of the patriarchs. The request displays Abraham buying the cave honestly. The verse displays the Machpelah: desired. The verse displays the money: full price. The verse displays the possession: sought. Abraham seeking to buy the cave of Machpelah at full price displays his honest dealing and faith: that he may give me the cave of Machpelah for full money, for a possession of a burying-place — the patriarch buying the cave (the future tomb of the patriarchs, 49:31) at its full value, as a secure possession, the first and only possession in the land of promise. The verse displays the requested cave, freely offered by Ephron (vv.10-11).
10:
"And Ephron dwelt among the children of Heth: and Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the audience of the children of Heth, even of all that went in at the gate of his city, saying," The brief verse notes Ephron answering Abraham publicly. The grammatical voices note the setting. Ellicott and Gill note that "Ephron dwelt among the children of Heth: and Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the audience of the children of Heth, even of all that went in at the gate of his city" — Ephron (the owner of the cave) was present among the Hittites, and answered Abraham publicly, in the hearing of all the people gathered at the city gate (the place of public business and witness); the public, formal negotiation; the transaction witnessed by the whole assembly.

On "in the audience of the children of Heth," the witnesses note the witnesses. Gill notes that the answer was given "in the audience" of the Hittites — publicly, before all; the transaction conducted openly, with many witnesses; the legal validity secured by the public dealing (the gate the place of legal business).

On "all that went in at the gate of his city," the grammatical-historical note the public place: "all that went in at the gate of his city" — the city gate, the place of public concourse, business, and judgment; the negotiation conducted there, before all the people; the public witness securing the purchase (vv.17-18); the formal, witnessed transaction. The verse displays Ephron answering Abraham publicly.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the setting — "Ephron... answered Abraham in the audience of the children of Heth," Ephron answering publicly before all (Ellicott, Gill); "in the audience," the transaction witnessed (Gill); "all that went in at the gate of his city," the public place of business (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the answering Ephron. The verse marks the Ephron: answering. The verse displays the audience: the Hittites. The verse displays the gate: the public place. The verse displays the witnesses: many. The setting displays the public, witnessed negotiation. The verse displays the Ephron: present. The verse displays the answer: public. The verse displays the gate: the place. Ephron answering Abraham publicly displays the formal, witnessed transaction: Ephron answered Abraham in the audience of the children of Heth, at the city gate — the public negotiation before all the people, securing the purchase by witness (vv.17-18). The verse displays Ephron's offer of the field and cave (v.11).
11:
"Nay, my lord, hear me: the field give I thee, and the cave that is therein, I give it thee; in the presence of the sons of my people give I it thee: bury thy dead." The witnesses note Ephron's offer to give Abraham the field and cave. On "the field give I thee, and the cave that is therein, I give it thee," the grammatical voices note the offer. Ellicott and Gill note that "the field give I thee, and the cave that is therein, I give it thee" — Ephron offered to give Abraham not only the cave but the whole field (a more generous offer, or an Eastern courtesy of seeming to give while expecting payment); the public profession of generosity; the offer of the field and cave as a gift.

On "in the presence of the sons of my people give I it thee," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the publicity. Calvin and Gill note that Ephron made the offer "in the presence of the sons of my people" — publicly, before witnesses; whether the offer was genuine generosity, or (as the Eastern manner) a polite form expecting payment (the naming of the price, v.15, suggests the latter); the public dealing.

On "bury thy dead," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the courtesy: "bury thy dead" — Ephron bade Abraham bury Sarah; the courteous granting of the burying-place; whether as a gift or a sale, the cave was to be Abraham's; the courteous offer that Abraham, in his integrity, would not accept as a gift but would pay for (vv.13-16). The verse displays Ephron's offer to give Abraham the field and cave.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the offer — "the field give I thee, and the cave that is therein, I give it thee," Ephron offering the field and cave (Ellicott, Gill); "in the presence of the sons of my people," the public offer (whether genuine or Eastern courtesy, Calvin, Gill); "bury thy dead," the courteous granting (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the offering Ephron. The verse marks the field: given. The verse displays the cave: given. The verse displays the offer: public. The verse displays the courtesy: shown. The offer displays Ephron seeming to give the field and cave. The verse displays the Ephron: offering. The verse displays the field: and cave. The verse displays the gift: professed. Ephron's offer to give Abraham the field and cave displays the public, courteous dealing: the field and the cave I give thee; bury thy dead — Ephron offering (whether in genuine generosity or Eastern courtesy) the field and cave, which Abraham would insist on buying (vv.13-16). The verse displays the offering Ephron, met by Abraham's insistence to pay (vv.12-13).
12:
"And Abraham bowed down himself before the people of the land." The very brief verse notes Abraham again bowing courteously to the people. The grammatical voices note the courtesy. Gill and Ellicott note that "Abraham bowed down himself before the people of the land" — Abraham again bowed (as v.7) in courteous acknowledgment of the Hittites' (and Ephron's) goodwill; the patriarch's politeness and humility; the gracious dealing of Abraham; the renewed courtesy before he insisted on paying (v.13).

On "bowed down himself," the witnesses note the humility. Gill notes that Abraham bowed in respect and gratitude; the humility of the great patriarch toward the people of the land; the courteous manners of the godly man (Romans 12:10).

On "before the people of the land," the grammatical-historical note the dealing: "before the people of the land" — Abraham's courteous bowing before the Hittites prepared for his renewed, firm request to buy the cave (v.13); the patriarch's gracious yet resolute dealing; the courtesy joined with integrity (insisting on paying). The verse displays Abraham again bowing courteously to the people.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the courtesy — "Abraham bowed down himself before the people of the land," Abraham's renewed courteous bowing (v.7, Gill, Ellicott); the humility of the patriarch (Romans 12:10, Gill); the courtesy preparing for his insistence to pay (v.13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bowing Abraham. The verse marks the bowing: again. The verse displays the people: honored. The verse displays the humility: shown. The verse displays the courtesy: renewed. The bowing displays Abraham's courteous acknowledgment. The patriarch, though a mighty prince among them (Genesis 23:6), bowed himself as a stranger and a sojourner (Genesis 23:4), joining greatness with lowliness, as the godly are called to honour all men and to condescend to men of low estate (1 Peter 2:17; Romans 12:16). The verse displays the Abraham: bowing. The verse displays the people: respected. The verse displays the courtesy: shown. Abraham again bowing courteously to the people displays his humility and grace: Abraham bowed before the people of the land — the patriarch's renewed courteous acknowledgment of their goodwill, before insisting on paying the full price (v.13). The verse displays the bowing Abraham, insisting on payment (v.13).
13:
"And he spake unto Ephron in the audience of the people of the land, saying, But if thou wilt give it, I pray thee, hear me: I will give thee money for the field; take it of me, and I will bury my dead there." The witnesses note Abraham insisting on paying for the field. On "I will give thee money for the field," the grammatical voices note the insistence. Ellicott and Gill note that "But if thou wilt give it, I pray thee, hear me: I will give thee money for the field" — Abraham insisted on paying for the field; he would not accept it as a gift (v.11), but would buy it at full price; the patriarch's integrity, that the possession might be securely and rightfully his; the firm, honest dealing.

On "take it of me," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the integrity. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham said, "take it of me" — pressing Ephron to accept payment; the scrupulous integrity of the patriarch, that he might owe nothing and hold the possession by right of purchase (against future dispute, as the seizing of his well, 21:25); the honest, careful dealing; the refusal to be enriched by another (cf. 14:23).

On "I will bury my dead there," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the purpose: "I will bury my dead there" — Abraham would bury Sarah in the field he bought; the secure burying-place; the possession rightfully purchased; the integrity that secured the tomb of the patriarchs (vv.17-20) by honest payment. The verse displays Abraham insisting on paying for the field.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the insistence — "I will give thee money for the field," Abraham insisting on paying, not taking a gift (Ellicott, Gill); "take it of me," the scrupulous integrity, holding by right of purchase (14:23; 21:25, Calvin, Gill); "I will bury my dead there," the secure burying-place (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the insisting Abraham. The verse marks the money: offered. The verse displays the field: to be bought. The verse displays the integrity: of Abraham. The verse displays the possession: secured. The insistence displays Abraham buying honestly. The verse displays the Abraham: insisting. The verse displays the payment: pressed. The verse displays the integrity: shown. Abraham insisting on paying for the field displays his integrity: I will give thee money for the field; take it of me — the patriarch refusing the gift and buying the field at full price, that the possession might be securely and rightfully his (14:23; 21:25). The verse displays the insisting Abraham, named the price by Ephron (vv.14-15).
14:
"And Ephron answered Abraham, saying unto him," The very brief verse introduces Ephron's naming of the price. The grammatical voices note the response. Gill and Henry note that "Ephron answered Abraham, saying unto him" — Ephron responded to Abraham's insistence on paying (v.13); the introduction to his naming of the price (v.15); the seller now stating the value of the field; the transaction proceeding to the price.

On "Ephron answered Abraham," the puritan Henry notes the turn. Henry and Gill note that Ephron, having offered the field as a "gift" (v.11), now (when Abraham insisted on paying) named its price (v.15); the Eastern manner of bargaining — the seeming gift, then the price; the seller's response, revealing the real expectation of payment.

On the answer, the grammatical-historical note the price to come: Ephron's answer (vv.14-15) names the value of the field (four hundred shekels); the seller stating the price, in the public dealing; the transaction proceeding to the payment and securing of the possession (v.16). The verse introduces Ephron's naming of the price.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the response — "Ephron answered Abraham, saying unto him," Ephron responding to Abraham's insistence (Gill, Henry); the turn from the seeming gift to the price (v.15, Henry, Gill); the naming of the value to come (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the answering Ephron. The verse marks the answer: of Ephron. The verse displays the price: to be named. The verse displays the bargaining: Eastern. The verse displays the transaction: proceeding. The answer displays Ephron about to name the price. The seeming gift now gives way to the price, after the manner of the East, that Abraham might buy the field outright and hold it as a sure possession (Genesis 23:17) against all future dispute, as he would owe nothing to any man (Genesis 14:23). The verse displays the Ephron: answering. The verse displays the price: forthcoming. The verse displays the deal: proceeding. Ephron's naming of the price displays the turn in the bargaining: Ephron answered Abraham — responding to the insistence on payment, about to name the value of the field (v.15), the Eastern manner of the seeming gift now revealing the price. The verse displays Ephron, naming four hundred shekels (v.15).
15:
"My lord, hearken unto me: the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver; what is that betwixt me and thee? bury therefore thy dead." The witnesses note Ephron naming the price of four hundred shekels. On "the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver," the grammatical voices note the price. Ellicott and Gill note that "the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver" — Ephron named the price: four hundred shekels of silver (a considerable sum); the value of the field stated; the seller, having professed to give it (v.11), now named a full (perhaps high) price; the transaction at the stated value.

On "what is that betwixt me and thee?" the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the manner. Clarke and Ellicott note that Ephron added, "what is that betwixt me and thee?" — a polite, dismissive phrase (as if the sum were trifling between friends); the Eastern courtesy of seeming to make light of the price while naming it; the manner of the bargaining; the seller's professed indifference to the (substantial) sum.

On "bury therefore thy dead," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the granting: "bury therefore thy dead" — Ephron bade Abraham bury Sarah, the field being his at the price; the granting of the burying-place upon the agreed value; the transaction concluded, awaiting Abraham's payment (v.16). The verse displays Ephron naming the price of four hundred shekels.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the price — "the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver," Ephron naming the price (Ellicott, Gill); "what is that betwixt me and thee?" the Eastern courtesy making light of the sum (Clarke, Ellicott); "bury therefore thy dead," the granting at the price (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the price named. The verse marks the price: 400 shekels. The verse displays the manner: dismissive. The verse displays the field: valued. The verse displays the granting: upon payment. The naming displays Ephron stating the value. The verse displays the Ephron: naming. The verse displays the shekels: four hundred. The verse displays the deal: set. Ephron naming the price of four hundred shekels displays the stated value: the land is worth four hundred shekels; what is that between us? bury thy dead — Ephron naming the (substantial) price with Eastern courtesy, the field to be Abraham's upon payment. The verse displays the named price, weighed out by Abraham (v.16).
16:
"And Abraham hearkened unto Ephron; and Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver, which he had named in the audience of the sons of Heth, four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant." The witnesses note Abraham weighing out the full price for the field. On "Abraham hearkened unto Ephron; and Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver," the grammatical voices note the payment. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham hearkened unto Ephron; and Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver, which he had named" — Abraham accepted the price and weighed out (in those days silver was weighed, not coined) the four hundred shekels; the full payment, without bargaining down; the patriarch's prompt, honest payment of the named price; the securing of the purchase.

On "in the audience of the sons of Heth," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the public transaction. Calvin and Gill note that the payment was made "in the audience of the sons of Heth" — publicly, before witnesses; the legal validity of the purchase secured by the public weighing of the money; the transaction witnessed and binding (vv.17-18); the careful, formal dealing.

On "four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the full price: "four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant" — the full, named price, in current (standard, accepted) money; Abraham paid the whole, in good silver; the honest, complete payment; the possession bought at full value, securely his (vv.17-20). The verse displays Abraham weighing out the full price.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the payment — "Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver... four hundred shekels," Abraham paying the full price (Ellicott, Gill); "in the audience of the sons of Heth," the public, witnessed transaction (Calvin, Gill); "current money with the merchant," the full price in good silver (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the paying Abraham. The verse marks the silver: weighed. The verse displays the price: 400 shekels. The verse displays the audience: the Hittites. The verse displays the payment: full. The payment displays Abraham buying the field at full price publicly. The verse displays the Abraham: paying. The verse displays the silver: weighed out. The verse displays the purchase: secured. Abraham weighing out the full price for the field displays his honest, secure purchase: Abraham weighed to Ephron four hundred shekels of silver, before the Hittites — the patriarch paying the full named price in good money, publicly, securing the possession (vv.17-20). The verse displays the bought field, made sure to Abraham (vv.17-18).
17:
"And the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah, which was before Mamre, the field, and the cave which was therein, and all the trees that were in the field, that were in all the borders round about, were made sure" The witnesses note the field and cave made sure to Abraham. On "the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah, which was before Mamre," the grammatical voices note the property. Ellicott and Gill note that "the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah, which was before Mamre, the field, and the cave which was therein, and all the trees" — the property is described in legal detail: the field in Machpelah, near Mamre (Hebron), with the cave and all the trees in its borders; the careful, full description of the purchase; the legal conveyance of the property.

On "all the trees that were in the field, that were in all the borders round about," the witnesses note the completeness. Gill notes the detailed enumeration (the field, the cave, the trees, the borders) — the full, legal description of the conveyed property; the completeness of the transaction; the careful securing of every part.

On "were made sure," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the conveyance: "were made sure" — the property was legally and securely conveyed (established, confirmed) to Abraham; the formal, witnessed transfer (v.18); the possession made sure and rightfully his (against future dispute); the first secure possession of Abraham in the land of promise (a burying-place). The verse displays the field and cave made sure to Abraham.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the property — "the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah... the field, and the cave... and all the trees," the full legal description (Ellicott, Gill); the completeness of the conveyance (Gill); "were made sure," the secure, legal conveyance to Abraham (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the conveyed property. The verse marks the field: Machpelah. The verse displays the cave: and trees. The verse displays the property: described. The verse displays the conveyance: secure. The transaction displays the property made sure to Abraham. The verse displays the field: conveyed. The verse displays the cave: secured. The verse displays the possession: sure. The field and cave made sure to Abraham display the secure, legal conveyance: the field, the cave, and all the trees were made sure to Abraham — the property legally and securely conveyed, the first secure possession of the patriarch in the land of promise. The verse displays the secured possession, in the presence of the Hittites (v.18).
18:
"Unto Abraham for a possession in the presence of the children of Heth, before all that went in at the gate of his city." The brief verse notes the possession secured to Abraham before witnesses. The grammatical voices note the conveyance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Unto Abraham for a possession in the presence of the children of Heth, before all that went in at the gate of his city" — the property was made sure (v.17) unto Abraham "for a possession" (his own, secure holding), publicly, in the presence of the Hittites and all who passed the city gate; the legal, witnessed conveyance; the possession formally and securely Abraham's.

On "for a possession," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the holding. Calvin and Gill note that the field was Abraham's "for a possession" — a secure, permanent holding; the first piece of the promised land the patriarch possessed (a burying-place, vv.4, 9); the pledge of the future inheritance; the possession by right of purchase.

On "in the presence of the children of Heth, before all that went in at the gate," the grammatical-historical note the witnesses: the conveyance was made publicly, before all the Hittites and all who passed the gate; the legal validity secured by the many witnesses; the transaction binding and unquestionable; the careful, formal securing of the possession; the first possession of Abraham in the land, openly and rightfully his. The verse displays the possession secured to Abraham before witnesses.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conveyance — "Unto Abraham for a possession in the presence of the children of Heth," the property secured to Abraham publicly (Keil, Gill); "for a possession," the secure, first holding in the land (vv.4, 9, Calvin, Gill); "before all that went in at the gate," the witnessed, binding transaction (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the secured possession. The verse marks the possession: Abraham's. The verse displays the witnesses: the Hittites. The verse displays the gate: the public place. The verse displays the conveyance: binding. The transaction displays the possession secured to Abraham. The verse displays the Abraham: possessing. The verse displays the witnesses: many. The verse displays the holding: secure. The possession secured to Abraham before witnesses displays the legal, binding conveyance: the field was made sure to Abraham for a possession, before all the Hittites at the gate — the first secure possession of the patriarch in the land of promise, openly and rightfully his. The verse displays the secured possession, in which Abraham buries Sarah (v.19).
19:
"And after this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre: the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan." The brief verse notes Abraham burying Sarah in the cave of Machpelah. The grammatical voices note the burial. Gill notes that "after this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre: the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan" — Abraham buried Sarah in the cave he had bought; the burial of the matriarch in the land of promise; the cave of Machpelah, at Hebron, now the tomb of Sarah (and later of Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, Leah, 49:31; 50:13); the patriarchal burying-place.

On "in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre," the witnesses note the place. Gill notes the place — the cave of Machpelah, near Mamre (Hebron); the secure burying-place, bought and rightfully Abraham's; the tomb of the patriarchs in the land.

On "in the land of Canaan," the grammatical-historical note the land of promise: "in the land of Canaan" — Sarah was buried in the land God had promised to Abraham's seed; the burial in the land of promise an act of faith and hope (the patriarchs buried in the land, in expectation of the inheritance and the resurrection); the matriarch laid in the promised land; the first burial in the patriarchal tomb. The verse displays Abraham burying Sarah in the cave of Machpelah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the burial — "Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre," Sarah buried in the bought cave, the patriarchal tomb (49:31, Gill); "in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre," the secure burying-place at Hebron (Gill); "in the land of Canaan," the burial in the land of promise, in faith (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the buried Sarah. The verse marks the burial: in Machpelah. The verse displays the cave: the tomb. The verse displays the Hebron: the place. The verse displays the land: of Canaan. The burial displays Sarah laid in the promised land. The verse displays the Sarah: buried. The verse displays the cave: her tomb. The verse displays the land: of promise. Abraham burying Sarah in the cave of Machpelah displays the burial in the land of promise: Abraham buried Sarah in the cave of Machpelah at Hebron, in the land of Canaan — the matriarch laid in the patriarchal tomb in the promised land, an act of faith and hope. The verse displays the buried Sarah, the possession confirmed (v.20).
20:
"And the field, and the cave that is therein, were made sure unto Abraham for a possession of a buryingplace by the sons of Heth." The witnesses note the field and cave confirmed to Abraham as a burying-place. On "the field, and the cave that is therein, were made sure unto Abraham," the grammatical voices note the confirmation. Ellicott and Gill note that "the field, and the cave that is therein, were made sure unto Abraham for a possession of a buryingplace by the sons of Heth" — the closing summary confirms that the field and cave were securely conveyed to Abraham, by the Hittites, as his possession and burying-place; the legal, binding transfer concluded; the possession made sure.

On "for a possession of a buryingplace," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the possession. Calvin and Gill note that the cave was Abraham's "for a possession of a buryingplace" — the secure, permanent burying-place of the patriarch and his house; the first possession in the land of promise (a grave, vv.4, 9); the pledge of the future inheritance; the burying-place of the patriarchs.

On "by the sons of Heth," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the close: "by the sons of Heth" — the conveyance confirmed by the Hittites (the witnesses and the seller); the legal, public securing of the possession; the close of the chapter; Abraham's faith — buying and possessing a burying-place in the land of promise, the pledge of the inheritance and the hope of the resurrection (Hebrews 11:13-16, 19); the first possession of the patriarch in the land. The verse displays the field and cave confirmed to Abraham as a burying-place.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the confirmation — "the field, and the cave that is therein, were made sure unto Abraham," the secure conveyance confirmed (Ellicott, Gill); "for a possession of a buryingplace," the first possession in the land, the patriarchal tomb (vv.4, 9, Calvin, Gill); "by the sons of Heth," the legal, public securing (Hebrews 11:13-16, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the confirmed possession. The verse marks the field: made sure. The verse displays the cave: the tomb. The verse displays the possession: Abraham's. The verse displays the close: confirmed. The summary displays the possession securely Abraham's. The verse displays the field: secured. The verse displays the burying-place: confirmed. The verse displays the possession: sure. The field and cave confirmed to Abraham as a burying-place display the secured possession and the patriarch's faith: the field and cave were made sure to Abraham for a burying-place by the sons of Heth — the first secure possession of the patriarch in the land of promise (a grave), the pledge of the inheritance and the hope of the resurrection (Hebrews 11:13-16, 19). The verse closes the chapter, before the marriage of Isaac (ch.24).
